blocks.content: About ten children sat at the table as Rumina's mother generously served them boiled meat.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Preparing this much food must've cost a fair amount. Paying for it wasn't an issue, but that would've been strange in itself.
As if reading his mind, Altol said, "Don't worry. We can sell the monster hides for money. We'll start tomorrow, with your permission, of course."

blocks.speaker: Altol
blocks.content: Don't worry. We can sell the monster hides for money. We'll start tomorrow, with your permission, of course.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Huh? Oh, of course. And the monsters are called Wulke. Their claws and fangs should fetch a good price.
Shirone had expected as much. Knowing other parts were valuable meant even greater profits.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: But more than that, he was glad to learn the monsters' name.
In this world, knowledge was also money.
Selling them without even knowing their name would've been a disaster. They might've lost more than the effort spent skinning them.

blocks.speaker: Altol
blocks.content: I see. Thanks. Without you, our village would've had to relocate.
For generations, they'd tilled the fields and sold forest goods. Losing their home would've been devastating.
In that sense, Shirone was the hero who'd saved the village.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: It's fine. This place is important to me too.
Shirone meant it. Even though the Wulke meat would spoil and become unsellable, the children had stayed instead of heading back to the mountains.
They weren't wealthy. Life was hard, and they blushed more often than nobles. But they knew how to live together, and he liked that.

blocks.speaker: Martin
blocks.content: Sorry your vacation turned out like this. Wanna come up the mountain tomorrow? We'll sell the hides and claws and have fun with the money.
The slash-and-burn kids never missed a chance to play, but Shirone regretfully declined.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: He wanted to return and analyze his newfound insights as soon as possible.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: No, I've rested enough. Thanks to you, I'm not tired anymore. I need to study hard now. I'll head back to school tomorrow morning.
After dinner, the children returned to their homes. Though the moon was out, they still had work to do—repairing gear and checking carts.
Shirone climbed the hill and looked down at the slash-and-burn village. The children carried torches, preparing for the next day's work while joking around.
Their cheerful banter brought a soft smile to his lips.

blocks.speaker: Altol
blocks.content: Shirone, there you are.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Huh? Altol, how'd you find me?

blocks.speaker: Altol
blocks.content: Just had a feeling. What's on your mind? Didn't even notice me coming.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Old memories. I used to run around here with my dad. Played tag in that field.

blocks.speaker: Altol
blocks.content: Yeah. But you spent way more time reading alone. Probably because of us.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: No, I just loved books…
Altol laughed warmly.

blocks.speaker: Altol
blocks.content: No need for that. It's obvious. The kids have always admired you. I was jealous sometimes. Looking back, I owe you more apologies than I can count.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: No, I was the odd one. Maybe I was scared of meeting people. It was a confusing time.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: In the slash-and-burn village, Shirone was the pride of his parents, unlike the other kids who caused trouble. But in truth, after discovering magic at twelve, his life had been endless turmoil.
Altol nodded knowingly.

blocks.speaker: Altol
blocks.content: The boy they'd thought was a bookish loner had been fighting harder than anyone.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Sorry, Shirone.

blocks.speaker: Altol
blocks.content: No, not that. At the drinking party. I overstepped. Everyone has their limits. I was wrong. Don't hold it against me.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Shirone recalled Altol's blunt words from last night. It hadn't hurt. It was just the burden he'd chosen by pursuing magic.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: I never thought about it. Honestly, I'd never realized how blessed I am. How lucky.

blocks.speaker: Altol
blocks.content: No, Shirone. You worked hard. That's why people help you.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: But it's the truth. What you said is my reality, something I'll carry forever.
Altol smiled bitterly.

blocks.speaker: Altol
blocks.content: Mages are something else.
Their way of thinking was incomprehensible. But perhaps that was why the world revered them.
Shirone and Altol gazed at the village. Though neither spoke, their eyes were fixed on the same sight.
The next morning.
The adults had started their day at dawn, leaving only the children behind.
Shirone ate breakfast at Rumina's house and prepared to leave.
The children saw him off, though they were laden with tools for dismantling the Wulke.
In their relentless lives, Shirone realized just how weighty his own path was.

blocks.speaker: Altol
blocks.content: Take care, Shirone. Study hard. Become a great mage.
Altol offered a handshake. The others crowded around, saying their goodbyes.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Shirone, you'll visit again, right?

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Tell us all about magic school next time.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Haha! Sure. I'll come back after the term ends. You all take care.
After bidding farewell, Shirone turned to Rumina. Amid the cheerful atmosphere, only she looked somber.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Shirone had an inkling of her feelings. But what could he do? Matters of the heart weren't decided by logic.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Rumina, thanks for all the delicious food. You'll cook for me again, right?

blocks.speaker: Rumina
blocks.content: Of course! So visit often. If you act all high and mighty as a mage, I'll beat you up.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Yeah. I'll definitely come back.
As Shirone descended the hill, he suddenly turned back.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Stay safe! Stay healthy!
When Shirone turned and walked away, Rumina finally hung her head.

blocks.speaker: Rumina
blocks.content: More painful than his departure was the realization that she could no longer love him.

blocks.speaker: Altol
blocks.content: Hey, you okay?
Altol placed a hand on her shoulder.

blocks.speaker: Rumina
blocks.content: Rumina couldn't hold back her tears.

blocks.speaker: Altol
blocks.content: Tch, if you're this upset, you should've said something. Want me to drag him back for you?

blocks.speaker: Rumina
blocks.content: No, it's fine. Shirone isn't really leaving.

blocks.speaker: Rumina
blocks.content: Rumina wiped her tears with both hands and looked up.

blocks.speaker: Rumina
blocks.content: Shirone is… chasing his dream.
Rumina prayed to the gods of the mountain and forest.
May warm light guide his path.
On his way back to school, Shirone pondered the questions the slash-and-burn village had raised.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Just how far could he go?
Becoming a guest of the Ozent family or studying with magic school students didn't mean he'd achieved anything.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: The advice to learn from failure and persevere didn't apply to a commoner like him.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: He had to explode his talent relentlessly. He couldn't afford to hesitate.
Thanks to the Ozent family's support, he attended school. His parents' sacrifices let him study advanced magic.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Was this the right path?

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: If he failed, could he accept it and live as before?
Arriving at school, Shirone headed to the research club.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Feeling unsettled, he didn't want to return to the dorm.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: 'Are the others having fun? I hope they didn't lose much…'

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: By now, Iruki and Nade were probably betting wildly at the casino.
He just hoped they hadn't wagered the house deeds.
Passing through the maze of Istas, Shirone stopped at the warehouse door.
Voices leaked through the gap.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Huh?
Inside, Iruki and Nade were arguing on the sofa.

blocks.speaker: Iruki
blocks.content: You idiot! A perpetual motion machine is impossible!

blocks.speaker: Nade
blocks.content: Why are you so closed-minded? I'm not talking about infinite energy but a second-kind perpetual motion machine!

blocks.speaker: Iruki
blocks.content: That violates the laws of entropy!

blocks.speaker: Nade
blocks.content: Then what's this? What do you call this?!
Nade pointed at a wind-up cart on the table.

blocks.speaker: Nade
blocks.content: Shirone! Tell him. I made this. You wind it up like this, then put it down…
The cart moved in circular motions, yet the winding didn't loosen.

blocks.speaker: Iruki
blocks.content: Hey, Shirone! Don't fall for it. It's a total scam!

blocks.speaker: Nade
blocks.content: What scam? You were waiting to see when it'd stop before you started this!

blocks.speaker: Iruki
blocks.content: Idiot! If it could last 1-2 hours, anyone could make one! Yours is just highly efficient! I tried winding it—it's ridiculously tight!

blocks.speaker: Nade
blocks.content: So what? It's a miniaturized version! A full-scale one would be 100 times more efficient!

blocks.speaker: Iruki
blocks.content: How big are you planning to make it? Sky-sized? Feeding a horse would be more efficient!

blocks.speaker: Nade
blocks.content: I just need to show it's possible! Technology trends toward miniaturization over time!

blocks.speaker: Iruki
blocks.content: Without theory, technology collapses!

blocks.speaker: Nade
blocks.content: Who cares about theory? Just make it and use it!
The mathematician and the engineer both had points.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Shirone blinked, listening, then examined the cart.
Watching them argue over a useless cart made his earlier worries vanish.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Ah… I see.
Someday, that cart would earn a proper name through scholars' research. And someone would use it to create new knowledge.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: How arrogant to think only he could do something.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: He hadn't come this far because he was special.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: If his father hadn't bought him books, if he hadn't joined the Ozent family, if there'd been no teleportation test, if he hadn't met Iruki and Nade…
Shirone wouldn't exist.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: A chain of tiny, improbable coincidences had led him here.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Thank you. For letting me be here.
Lost in emotion, Shirone quickly smiled and asked his friends, "So, how was the casino?"

blocks.speaker: Nade
blocks.content: How? We lost everything in one day and came back. One more bet, and we'd have hit the jackpot.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: I told you it'd be Spades. A 57% chance.

blocks.speaker: Nade
blocks.content: Funny! I listened to you and lost a ton! 57% my foot! How's that different from gambling?
